title: Apply: Punnett squares
description: Apply your understanding of Punnett squares in this set of free practice questions aligned to NGSS standards.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a population of pea plants, some plants have tall stems and others have short stems. The gene for stem height has two possible alleles. One allele is for tall stems (*T*), and the other allele is for short stems (*t*).

Consider a cross between two pea plants. One of the plants is homozygous for a tall stem (*TT*). The other plant is homozygous for a short stem (*tt*).

**Which Punnett square correctly models the results of this cross?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To determine which Punnett square is correct, let’s check each square's parent and offspring genotypes.

Parent genotypes are located on the outside of a Punnett square. One parent’s alleles are placed on top, and the other parent’s alleles are placed along the left side.

For a cross between one parent with the *TT* genotype, and another parent with the *tt* genotype, the Punnett square would be set up like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

Offspring genotypes are placed in the boxes within a Punnett square. Each box contains a combination of parent alleles: one copy of the parent allele found above the box, and one copy of the parent allele found to the side of the box.

The following diagram shows how to determine the offspring genotype for the top-right box of the Punnett square. (Note that genotypes are always written with the dominant allele first.)

[[☃ image 1]]

We can use this same process to figure out the correct offspring genotypes for the other three boxes.

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:

Consider a cross between two *Hibiscus* plants. One of the plants is homozygous for red flowers (*FF*). The other plant is heterozygous (*Ff*).

**Which Punnett square correctly models the results of this cross?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To determine which Punnett square is correct, let’s check each square's parent and offspring genotypes.

Parent genotypes are located on the outside of a Punnett square. One parent’s alleles are placed on top, and the other parent’s alleles are placed along the left side.

For a cross between one parent with the *FF* genotype, and another parent with the *Ff* genotype, the Punnett square would be set up like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

Offspring genotypes are placed in the boxes within a Punnett square. Each box contains a combination of parent alleles: one copy of the parent allele found above the box, and one copy of the parent allele found to the side of the box.

The following diagram shows how to determine the offspring genotype for the top right box of the Punnett square.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can use this same process to figure out the correct offspring genotypes for the other three boxes. (Note that genotypes are always written with the dominant allele first.)

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
In a population of cows, some individuals have a black coat and others have a red coat. The gene for coat color has two possible alleles. One allele is for a black coat (*B*), and the other allele is for a red coat (*b*).

Consider a cross between two cows. Both cows are heterozygous for the coat color gene (*Bb*).

**Which Punnett square correctly models the results of this cross?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: To determine which Punnett square is correct, let’s check each square's parent and offspring genotypes.

Parent genotypes are located on the outside of a Punnett square. One parent’s alleles are placed on top, and the other parent’s alleles are placed along the left side.

For a cross between two parents with the *Bb* genotype, the Punnett square would be set up like this:

[[☃ image 1]]

Offspring genotypes are placed in the boxes within a Punnett square. Each box contains a combination of parent alleles: one copy of the parent allele found above the box, and one copy of the parent allele found to the side of the box.

The following diagram shows how to determine the offspring genotype for the top right box of the Punnett square.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can use this same process to figure out the correct offspring genotypes for the other three boxes. (Note that genotypes are always written with the dominant allele first.)

The correct answer is:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
In a population of cats, some individuals have short fur and others have long fur. The gene for fur length has two possible alleles. One allele is for short fur (*F*), and the other allele is for long fur (*f*).

The Punnett square below models the results of a cross between two cats. Both cats are heterozygous for the fur length gene. 

One of the boxes in the Punnett square has been left empty.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which offspring genotype should go in the empty box?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Each box in a Punnett square contains a combination of parent alleles: one copy of the parent allele found above the box, and one copy of the parent allele found to the side of the box.

So, the Punnett square’s empty box should contain one copy of the *F* allele, and one copy of the *f* allele, as shown in the diagram below.

[[☃ image 1]]
The offspring genotype that should go in the Punnett square’s empty box is *Ff*.

options.alt: A Punnett square shows a square with four boxes. Across the top of the square from left to right are uppercase F and lowercase f. Along the left side of the square from top to bottom are uppercase F and lowercase f. The top-right box of the Punnett square is empty. The other boxes are filled in with the following genotypes. Top-left box: uppercase F and uppercase F. Bottom-left box: uppercase F and lowercase f. Bottom-right box: lowercase f and lowercase f.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a population of snails, some individuals have solid shells, and others have striped shells. The gene for shell type has two possible alleles. One allele is for a solid shell (*B*), and the other allele is for a striped shell (*b*).

The Punnett square below models the results of a cross between two snails. One of the snails is homozygous for a solid shell (*BB*). The other snail is homozygous for a striped shell (*bb*). 

One of the boxes in the Punnett square has been left empty.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which offspring genotype should go in the empty box?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Each box in a Punnett square contains a combination of parent alleles: one copy of the parent allele found above the box, and one copy of the parent allele found to the side of the box.

So, the Punnett square’s empty box should contain one copy of the *B* allele, and one copy of the *b* allele, as shown in the diagram below. (Note that genotypes are always written with the dominant allele first.)

[[☃ image 1]]
The offspring genotype that should go in the Punnett square’s empty box is *Bb*.

options.alt: A Punnett square shows a square with four boxes. Across the top of the square from left to right are uppercase B and uppercase B. Along the left side of the square from top to bottom are lowercase b and lowercase b. The bottom-left box of the Punnett square is empty. The other boxes are filled in with the following genotypes. Top-left box: uppercase B and lowercase b. Top-right box: uppercase B and lowercase b. Bottom-right box: uppercase B and lowercase b.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a population of koi fish, some individuals have red eyes and others have black eyes. The gene for eye color has two possible alleles. One allele is for red eyes (*E*), and the other allele is for black eyes (*e*).

The Punnett square below models the results of a cross between two koi fish. One of the fish is homozygous for black eyes (*ee*). The other fish is heterozygous (*Ee*).

One of the boxes in the Punnett square has been left empty.

[[☃ image 1]]


**Which offspring genotype should go in the empty box?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Each box in a Punnett square contains a combination of parent alleles: one copy of the parent allele found above the box, and one copy of the parent allele found to the side of the box.

So, the Punnett square’s empty box should contain two copies of the *e* allele, as shown here: 

[[☃ image 1]]
The offspring genotype that should go in the Punnett square’s empty box is *ee*.

options.alt: A Punnett square shows a square with four boxes. Across the top of the square from left to right are lowercase e and lowercase e. Along the left side of the square from top to bottom are uppercase E and lowercase e. The bottom-right box of the Punnett square is empty. The other boxes are filled in with the following genotypes. Top-left box: uppercase E and lowercase e. Bottom-left box: lowercase e and lowercase e. Top-right box: uppercase E and lowercase e.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a population of pea plants, some plants have yellow seeds and others have green seeds. The gene for seed color has two possible alleles. One allele is for yellow seeds (*Y*), and the other allele is for green seeds (*y*).

The Punnett square below models the results of a cross between two pea plants. One of the plants is homozygous for green seeds (*yy*). The other plant is heterozygous (*Yy*).

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the expected ratio of offspring with yellow seeds to offspring with green seeds?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A Punnett square shows the likelihood of offspring genotypes resulting from a cross. We can use these *genotypes* to figure out the expected ratio of offspring *phenotypes*.

Two boxes in the Punnett square have the genotype *Yy*. This genotype has one allele for yellow seeds, which is the dominant allele. So, two boxes in the Punnett square represent offspring with yellow seeds.

[[☃ image 1]]

Two boxes in the Punnett square have the genotype *yy*. This genotype has two alleles for green seeds. So, two boxes in the Punnett square represent offspring with green seeds.

[[☃ image 1]]

Taken together, this means that the expected ratio of offspring with yellow seeds to offspring with green seeds is $2:2$. However, this ratio can be simplified.
The expected ratio of offspring with yellow seeds to offspring with green seeds is $1:1$.

options.alt: A Punnett square shows a square with four boxes filled in with genotypes. Across the top of the square from left to right are lowercase y and lowercase y. Along the left side of the square from top to bottom are uppercase Y and lowercase y. The filled-in boxes of the square show the following genotypes. Top-left box: uppercase Y and lowercase y. Top-right box: uppercase Y and lowercase y. Bottom-left box: lowercase y and lowercase y. Bottom-right box: lowercase y and lowercase y.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a population of jaguars, some individuals have a solid black coat and others have a spotted coat. The gene for coat type has two possible alleles. One allele is for a solid black coat (*B*), and the other allele is for a spotted coat (*b*).

The Punnett square below models the results of a cross between two jaguars. Both jaguars are heterozygous for the coat type gene (*Bb*).

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the expected ratio of offspring with a solid black coat to offspring with a spotted coat?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A Punnett square shows the likelihood of offspring genotypes resulting from a cross. We can use these *genotypes* to figure out the expected ratio of offspring *phenotypes*.

One box in the Punnett square has the genotype *BB*. This genotype has two alleles for a solid black coat. Two boxes in the Punnett square have the genotype *Bb*. This genotype has one allele for a solid black coat, which is the dominant allele. 

So, a total of three boxes in the Punnett square represent offspring with a solid black coat.

[[☃ image 1]]

One box in the Punnett square has the genotype *bb*.This genotype has two alleles for a spotted coat. So, one box in the Punnett square represents offspring with a spotted coat.

[[☃ image 1]]

Taken together, this means that for every three offspring with a solid black coat, we would expect there to be about one offspring with a spotted coat.
The expected ratio of offspring with a solid black coat to offspring with a spotted coat is $3:1$.

options.alt: A Punnett square shows a square with four boxes filled in with genotypes. Across the top of the square from left to right are uppercase B and lowercase b. Along the left side of the square from top to bottom are uppercase B and lowercase b. The filled-in boxes of the square show the following genotypes. Top-left box: uppercase B and uppercase B. Top-right box: uppercase B and lowercase b. Bottom-left box: uppercase B and lowercase b. Bottom-right box: lowercase b and lowercase b.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a population of hamsters, some individuals have straight fur and others have wavy fur. The gene for fur texture has two possible alleles. One allele is for straight fur (*F*), and the other allele is for wavy fur (*f*).

The Punnett square below models the results of a cross between two hamsters. Both hamsters are heterozygous for the coat texture gene (*Ff*).

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the expected ratio of offspring with wavy fur to offspring with straight fur?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A Punnett square shows the likelihood of offspring genotypes resulting from a cross. We can use these *genotypes* to figure out the expected ratio of offspring *phenotypes*.

One box in the Punnett square has the genotype *ff*. This genotype has two alleles for wavy fur. So, one box in the Punnett square represents offspring with wavy fur.

[[☃ image 1]]

One box in the Punnett square has the genotype *FF*. This genotype has two alleles for straight fur. Two boxes in the Punnett square have the genotype *Ff*. This genotype has one allele for straight fur, which is the dominant allele. 

So, a total of three boxes in the Punnett square represent offspring with straight fur.

[[☃ image 1]]

Taken together, this means that for every one offspring with wavy fur, we would expect there to be about three offspring with straight fur.
The expected ratio of offspring with wavy fur to offspring with straight fur is $1:3$.

options.alt: A Punnett square shows a square with four boxes filled in with genotypes. Across the top of the square from left to right are uppercase F and lowercase f. Along the left side of the square from top to bottom are uppercase F and lowercase f. The filled-in boxes of the square show the following genotypes. Top-left box: uppercase F and uppercase F. Top-right box: uppercase F and lowercase f. Bottom-left box: uppercase F and lowercase f. Bottom-right box: lowercase f and lowercase f.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a population of catfish, some fish have a brown body and others have a white body. The gene for body color has two possible alleles. One allele is for a brown body (*B*), and the other allele is for a white body (*b*).

The Punnett square below models the results of a cross between two catfish. One of the catfish is homozygous for a brown body (*BB*). The other catfish is heterozygous (*Bb*).

[[☃ image 1]]

**Approximately what fraction of offspring from this cross will have a brown body?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A Punnett square shows the likelihood of offspring genotypes resulting from a cross. We can use these *genotypes* to estimate the fraction of offspring with certain *phenotypes*.

Two boxes in the Punnett square have the genotype *BB*. This genotype has two alleles for a brown body.  The two other boxes in the Punnett square have the genotype *Bb*. This genotype has one allele for a brown body, which is the dominant allele. 

So, four out of four boxes in the Punnett square represent offspring with a brown body.

[[☃ image 1]]
The fraction of offspring from the cross that will have a brown body is approximately $\dfrac{4}{4}$.

options.alt: A Punnett square shows a square with four boxes filled in with genotypes. Across the top of the square from left to right are uppercase B and uppercase B. Along the left side of the square from top to bottom are uppercase B and lowercase b. The filled-in boxes of the square show the following genotypes. Top-left box: uppercase B and uppercase B. Top-right box: uppercase B and uppercase B. Bottom-left box: uppercase B and lowercase b. Bottom-right box: uppercase B and lowercase b.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a population of chickens, some individuals have white legs and others have yellow legs. The gene for leg color has two possible alleles. One allele is for white legs (*W*), and the other allele is for yellow legs (*w*).

The Punnett square below models the results of a cross between two chickens. One of the chickens is homozygous for yellow legs (*ww*). The other chicken is heterozygous (*Ww*).

[[☃ image 1]]

**Approximately what fraction of offspring from this cross will have yellow legs?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A Punnett square shows the likelihood of offspring genotypes resulting from a cross. We can use these *genotypes* to estimate the fraction of offspring with certain *phenotypes*.

Two boxes in the Punnett square have the genotype *ww*. This genotype has two alleles for yellow legs. The other two boxes in the Punnett square have the genotype *Ww*, which corresponds to the white leg phenotype. 

This means that two out of four boxes in the Punnett square represent offspring with yellow legs.

[[☃ image 1]]

So, the fraction of offspring from this cross that will have yellow legs is approximately $\dfrac{2}{4}$. However, this fraction can be simplified.
The fraction of offspring from the cross that will have yellow legs is approximately $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

options.alt: A Punnett square shows a square with four boxes filled in with genotypes. Across the top of the square from left to right are uppercase W and lowercase w. Along the left side of the square from top to bottom are lowercase w and lowercase w. The filled-in boxes of the square show the following genotypes. Top-left box: uppercase W and lowercase w. Top-right box: lowercase w and lowercase w. Bottom-left box: uppercase W and lowercase w. Bottom-right box: lowercase w and lowercase w.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a population of dogs, some individuals have black fur and others have brown fur. The gene for fur color has two possible alleles. One allele is for black fur (*F*), and the other allele is for brown fur (*f*).

The Punnett square below models the results of a cross between two dogs. Both dogs are heterozygous for the fur color gene (*Ff*).

[[☃ image 1]]

**Approximately what fraction of offspring from this cross will have black fur?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A Punnett square shows the likelihood of offspring genotypes resulting from a cross. We can use these *genotypes* to estimate the fraction of offspring with certain *phenotypes*.

One box in the Punnett square has the genotype *FF*. This genotype has two alleles for black fur. Two other boxes in the Punnett square have the genotype *Ff*. This genotype has one allele for black fur, which is the dominant allele.

The fourth box in the Punnett square has the genotype *ff*, which corresponds to the brown fur phenotype.

So, three out of four boxes in the Punnett square represent offspring with black fur.

[[☃ image 1]]
The fraction of offspring from the cross that will have black fur is approximately $\dfrac{3}{4}$.

Along the left side of the square from top to bottom are uppercase F and lowercase f. The filled-in boxes of the square show the following genotypes. Top-left box: uppercase F and uppercase F. Top-right box: uppercase F and lowercase f. Bottom-left box: uppercase F and lowercase f. Bottom-right box: lowercase f and lowercase f.
